docs.title: Who is this guide for?📘
docs.text:
This guide is intended for users getting started with ARCHIMEDES. It provides step-by-step instructions on account creation, data access, data transfer, data release, and how to search and analyze data within the platform.

For more information, refer to the FAQ or Data Governance Framework

docs.title: What is ARCHIMEDES?
docs.text:
ARCHIMEDES (Advanced Research Collaboration for Health Integration, MEDical Exploration, and data Synthesis) is a platform that enables researchers to store, manage, share, and access highly structured health-related research data. It also provides a High-Performance Computing (HPC) environment, through which users can analyze ARCHIMEDES data.

All data available on ARCHIMEDES are coded and/or de-identified prior to upload to ensure privacy and compliance.

docs.title: Why use ARCHIMEDES? 🤝
docs.text:
Health research is often slowed down by fragmented data sources, complex infrastructure, and limited access to high-quality datasets.

➡️ ARCHIMEDES brings everything together in one secure platform—data, governance, and analysis tools—so researchers can work more efficiently and collaboratively.

➡️ This integrated environment reduces the time and effort spent on data management and technical setup, allowing researchers to focus on analysis and discovery.

Within ARCHIMEDES, you can:

🧬 Access curated, multi-modal health datasets in one place ⚙️ Analyze data securely within an integrated HPC environment 🔐 Reduce technical and administrative barriers to research 🤝 Improve reproducibility and collaboration across teams

By simplifying how health data is accessed and used, ARCHIMEDES enables you to focus on advancing research and generating meaningful impact on health.

🧭 Our vision is to transform the Canadian health data landscape by building a bilingual national platform for the curation and reuse of health-related research data. ARCHIMEDES provides secure and flexible access to health data, enabling collaboration and innovation at a national scale.

docs.title: What types of data are hosted? 🧬
docs.text: ARCHIMEDES hosts curated, multi-modal health research, wearable, omics data (genomics, proteomics, transcriptomics, metabolomics, etc.) and summary biospecimen data. This includes both data from human research participants and non-human research data (i.e., pre-clinical data).

docs.title: How can you access data? 🔐
docs.text:
ARCHIMEDES uses a flexible access system that allows data contributors to decide how their datasets are shared and accessed. Data on ARCHIMEDES is presently shared through two tiers:

1. Controlled Access 🔒 ➡️ Data are exclusively made available to Principal Investigators (PIs) and their Research Team, for an approved research purpose. Applicants must first obtain approval from the ARCHIMEDES Data Access Committee (DAC) and enter into a Data Access Agreement (DAA).

ℹ️: DARs can only be submitted by Principal Investigators (PI)

2. Open Access 🔓 ➡️ Data are made openly available to anyone through ARCHIMEDES, on a public website. In this first phase of ARCHIMEDES, only pre-clinical (i.e., non-human) data are available via Open Access.

A third tier, Registered Data Access, will be available for ARCHIMEDES users in the next phase of the platform’s development.

docs.title: How does ARCHIMEDES work? ⚙️
docs.text:
ARCHIMEDES supports multiple data types that can be ingested and harmonized within the platform. Once available, data can be securely accessed and analyzed using integrated tools for processing, data analysis, and visualization.

➡️ The platform also provides a High-Performance Computing (HPC) environment, allowing users to perform analyses without requiring external infrastructure.

The schematic below illustrates the platform’s structure and capabilities.

Get Started

docs.title: Account Creation
docs.text:
Creating an ARCHIMEDES account will allow you to manage your profile, contribute data, access to controlled data and initiate data release. The process for requesting an account on ARCHIMEDES varies depending on whether you are a Principal Investigator (PI) or a research project team member.

In this section, we will guide you step by step on how to set-up an ARCHIMEDES account to access the platform.

docs.title: Create an account as a Principal Investigator
docs.text:
➡️ Step 1: Go to the Get Started page and Click on Request Account

➡️ A new window will open with the Request Account form.

➡️ Step 2: Fill the form and agree to the Terms of Use (Details available by clicking on the link)

➡️ Step 3: You will receive an email confirmation of your account request.

➡️ Step 4: Check your institutional inbox.

  • If your institution already has a DCA, the ARCHIMEDES team will provide instructions on activating your account.
  • If your institution does not yet have a DCA, the ARCHIMEDES team will work with your institution's legal signing authority to establish one before your account can be approved.

docs.title: Get access as a non‐Principal Investigator
docs.text: Users who are not Principal Investigators (PIs) cannot create an ARCHIMEDES account independently. To obtain access, they must be invited by a PI and receive an invitation code to join an existing project. After joining the PI’s project, invited users will be able to either contribute data to the project or access controlled data, depending on the permissions assigned to them.

docs.title: Create a new project and contribute data
docs.text:
Once your account creation is approved, you can begin the process of creating a new project. ARCHIMEDES supports two types of data contributions:

  • Coded data: Data subject to (i) an ethics approval and informed consent that authorize sharing; (ii) an ethics waiver of informed consent requirement, or (iii) another legal authorization. All direct identifiers (e.g., names, social insurance numbers) must be removed and replaced with a unique code prior to contribution.

  • De-identified data: Data can be shared if it is de-identified (or local equivalent). This requires that all direct identifiers and most indirect identifiers must be removed or transformed to reduce the risk of re-identification to a very low level, in accordance with recognized standards and applicable legal and ethical requirements.

There are two main steps to create a new project, submit a Data Contribution Form (DCF) and Create a New Project in ARCHIMEDES.

docs.title: Submit a Data Contribution Form (DCF)
docs.text:
➡️ From the dashboard, go to Project Requests and click on Create New Project.

➡️ A Create New Project window will open, asking you to enter the study name and the Principal Investigator’s (PI’s) information.

ℹ️Note: PI's information is autopopulated based on the account information. If the information you see is incorrect, you can modify it in the "my preferences", section available under your name.

➡️ Click Next.

➡️ Agree to the Data Contributor Terms of Use and click on Agree & Create New Project.

➡️ The project will be registered and a confirmation window will open displaying a unique Request ID and a link to complete the Data Contribution Form (DCF). A confirmation email will also be sent containing this information.

➡️ Complete the DCF MS form link.

➡️ Send The DCF (pdf format) with the requested supporting material to the Data Access Committee Office (DACO) at archimedes.daco@ottawaheart.ca.

➡️ This New Project will be pending until the DACO uploads the approved DCF.

➡️ From the dashboard, you can track the status of your project request under Project Requests. Click on the status to view additional details.

➡️ Approval status will be updated in a timely matter.

➡️ Once the DCF is approved by the DACO, click on the status (DCF Approved) and proceed to the next step.

docs.title: Create a New Project
docs.text: Complete the following steps to provide the required project information:

docs.title: 🗂️ Project information
docs.text:
In this section, you will be asked to provide general information about your project, including the study design, study population, expected number of participants, participant groups, contact information, institutional information, session information, and supporting documentation such as the study protocol.

💡Tip: a tooltip will appear when you hover over the icon

💡Tip: Health data may contain direct identifiers (e.g., names, health card numbers, exact addresses), indirect identifiers (e.g., date of birth, postal code, rare diagnoses), or embedded identifiers (e.g., metadata in imaging files or text within images). These elements must be carefully reviewed and either removed or modified to protect privacy.

➡️ According to ARCHIMEDES Data Submission, Date of Birth (DOB) is required as it supports data validation, structuring, and analysis (e.g., age consistency and longitudinal tracking). While the full format MM-DD-YYYY is preferred, users may alternatively provide MM-YYYY or YYYY when full DOB is not available or cannot be shared. If the year is not available, users may provide age only. In these cases, the system applies a default setting that calculates a standardized DOB based on the available information (month/year or age) to ensure consistency within the database structure, while still treating DOB as an indirect identifier and maintaining privacy protections.

💡Note: A ❌ indicates missing required information

Once all required information has been provided, a ✅ will appear.

docs.title: 🔬 Data Types
docs.text:
Most studies include multiple data type. Select all that apply from the drop down menu.

Once you have selected all data types included in your project, you will have to complete a separate form for each one. The example below shows the Imaging form, which is required when Imaging is selected as a data type.

💡 The descriptions below provide examples of the types of data that may be collected, generated, or analyzed as part of your research.

docs.title: 🧠 Behavioural
docs.text:
• Participant actions, habits, or self-reported experiences
  • Examples: surveys, questionnaires, cognitive tests, activity logs

docs.title: 🖼️ Imaging
docs.text:
• Visual or radiological data capturing anatomy or function
  • Examples: MRI, CT scans, X-rays, ultrasound, PET scans

docs.title: 🧪 Biospecimen
docs.text:
• Biological samples collected for analysis
  • Examples: blood, tissue, saliva, urine

docs.title: 🧬 -Omics
docs.text:
• Molecular or genomic data derived from DNA or RNA
  • Examples: whole genome sequencing, genotyping, gene expression data

docs.title: ⌚🔄 Wearables and continuous monitoring
docs.text:
• Data collected over time via devices or sensors
  • Examples: heart rate, sleep patterns, activity tracking from smartwatches or wearable sensors

docs.title: 📏 Other Measurements
docs.text:
• Additional clinical or physiological data not included above
  • Examples: vital signs (blood pressure, temperature), lab results, height, weight

docs.title: ✅ Proceed to the next steps until Status is completed
docs.text:
⏳ Your project request is now under administrative review.

If additional information is required, the ARCHIMEDES team will contact you. Once approved, you will receive an email outlining the next steps.

docs.title: Data Access
docs.text:
Data submitted to ARCHIMEDES will be made available either as Open Access or through a Controlled Access tier.

The access level selected by the data contributor must be based on the authorizations associated with the data. This includes providing an appropriate ethical and legal justification for sharing, such as research participant consent, an ethics waiver of informed consent, or other applicable legal authority for data release

Currently, data is shared through two access tiers:

docs.title: Open Access 🔓
docs.text:
Data is publicly available through ARCHIMEDES.

  • No account is required to view or use Open Access data
  • Users must agree to the Terms of Use before accessing the data
  • The Terms require that data is used only for research purposes and in compliance with ethical, legal, and governance requirements

docs.title: Controlled Access 🔒
docs.text:
Data is available only to authorized users.

  • Users must create an account and submit a Data Access Request (DAR) for the data they need
  • The request is reviewed by the Data Access Committee (DAC)
  • The DAC verifies the applicant and approves or denies access

ℹ️ Note: Only Principal Investigators (PIs) can submit DARs.

➡️ Coming Soon

A third tier, Registered Data Access, will be introduced in a future phase of the platform.

docs.title: Coming Soon
docs.text: This page is currently under development.

docs.title: Portal Navigation
docs.text:
In this section, we’ll introduce the main features that help you navigate the platform quickly and easily.

ARCHIMEDES is organized into sections accessible from the top menu. Hovering over a menu item reveals its subsections, and you can return to the Dashboard at any time by clicking ARCHIMEDES home icon. If you ever feel unsure about a feature, you can click the question mark icon to access Help Text for guidance.

We’ll cover the Dashboard, Navigation Path, Help Text, and Data Tables.

docs.title: Dashboard 📊
docs.text:
ARCHIMEDES is divided into sections, which are organized in a menu along the top of your screen. The sub sections details will appear as you hover over any of these menu items. At any point, you can click on ARCHIMEDES or the home icon to go back to the Dashboard.

💡 Tip: If you come across any page that does not display fully, navigate to your browser’s zoom function and zoom in or out. This may be the quick fix to any display issues.

Your dashboard is the first thing you see whenever logging into ARCHIMEDES. What you see on that page will depend on your role and permissions.

SCREENSHOT TO BE UPDATED

From the dashboard you can easily view:

  • 📁 My Projects – the project(s) you are affiliated with will be listed there.
  • 📚 Data Requests and Project creation – Access your submitted data requests or start a new project.
  • ✅ My tasks – View items assigned to you (data entry conflicts, issues, incomplete forms, etc.)
  • 📈 Study Progression – A timeline of scans collected by each study site, or a timeline of participant recruitment per site. Zoom in on the chart to view detailed data points.

tbd 🔎 Navigate the Graph Zoom and move within the graph by clicking and dragging with your mouse. Adjust the view until you see the time range or data you need. 📊 Change the Graph View Use the Views drop-down menu to switch between: 📈 View scans per site – see the number of scans collected at each site 👥 View recruitment per site – monitor participant recruitment by site 🏥 Show or Hide Sites Click on site names below the graph to toggle them on or off. This allows you to focus on specific sites.

🎯 Filter the Data – Some dashboard sections provide filters to refine the information displayed:

  • 📁 Project filter – select a specific project
  • 📂 Subproject filter – narrow results to a subproject
  • 📍 Site filter – view data for selected sites only

docs.title: Navigation path 🧭
docs.text:
The ARCHIMEDES website shows your current location below the menu bar using a Navigation Path. This path displays the sequence of pages you’ve navigated, helping you see where you are and quickly return to previous section

This path helps you understand where you are and quickly move to previous sections.

📍 Example Navigation Path

Home > Participant list > Participant #####/FDGP00000

In this example, the path shows that you are viewing a specific participant profile data within the Participant section.

docs.title: Help Text ❓
docs.text:
Every page in ARCHIMEDES includes a Help panel identified by a ❓ that explains the current section and how to use it.

❓ Open the Help Panel ➡️ Click the question mark icon (?) in the top-right corner of the page. The Help text panel will appear with instructions for that page.

📖 The Help panel provides: ➡️ 🧩 Section overview – a brief explanation of the current section ➡️ 📝 Detailed instructions – guidance on how to use the page ➡️ 📋 Step-by-step actions – instructions for completing tasks

❌ Close the Help Panel ➡️ Click Close to hide the Help panel when you are finished.

docs.title: Data Tables 📋
docs.text:
Many sections in ARCHIMEDES retrieve information from the database and display it in data tables. These tables include several tools that help you filter, organize, and export your data.

🔎 Filter Your Data ➡️ Use the Selection Filter section to narrow down the data displayed in the table. ➡️ Enter values in one or more filter fields to refine results. ➡️ As you type, the system automatically suggests matching data. ➡️ Click Clear Filters at any time to reset all filters.

⚠️ : When selecting multiple filter values, the system uses AND logic, not OR.

Example: PSCID: A AND B This means results must match all selected values.

⚙️ Advanced Filters Some tables include additional filtering options. ➡️ Click Show Advanced Filters: Additional filter fields will appear.

These allow you to further refine your search results.

↕️ Sort Table Columns You can easily sort the data displayed in the table. ➡️ Click any column header to sort by that column. ➡️ Click again to toggle between: - Ascending order - Descending order

📄 Adjust the Number of Rows ➡️ If the table contains many results, you can change how many rows appear per page. ➡️ Use the rows-per-page drop-down menu to increase or decrease the number of displayed rows.

⬇️ Download Table Data Most tables include a Download Table as CSV option. ➡️ Click this button to export the table data as a .csv file, which can be opened in spreadsheet software.

docs.title: Coming Soon
docs.text: This page is currently under development.

docs.title: Establish a Data Contribution Agreement (DCA)
docs.text:
Principal Investigators (PIs) can contribute data to ARCHIMEDES by requesting Data Contribution Permissions. As a first step, organizations must enter into a Data Contribution Agreement (DCA). If your organization does not yet have a DCA, the ARCHIMEDES team will work with you to establish one. If you have any questions, please don't hesitate to contact the ARCHIMEDES team at ARCHIMEDES@ottawaheart.ca

⚠️ No account creation or data submission can occur before the DCA is agreed to.

docs.title: ➡️ Data Contribution Agreement (DCA)
docs.text:
This agreement is established once per organization and will provide contribution permissions to chosen Principal Invetigators (PIs) from that organization. The Data Contribution Agreement (DCA) defines responsibilities, permissions, and governance requirements. Your organization must enter into a DCA before you can contribute data to ARCHIMEDES. If your organization does not yet have a DCA, the ARCHIMEDES team will work with you to establish one. If you have any questions, please don't hesitate to contact the ARCHIMEDES team at ARCHIMEDES@ottawaheart.ca

⚠️ No account creation or data submission can occur before the DCA is agreed to.

Once a Data Contribution Agreement (DCA) is in place:

  1. Your institution approves designated Principal Investigators (PIs).
  2. Approved PIs create an ARCHIMEDES account.
  3. Before proceeding, users must agree to the Terms of Use and confirm that all contributed data will be handled and shared in accordance with:
    • Ethical approvals ✅
    • Legal requirements ⚖️
    • ARCHIMEDES governance policies 🏛️

docs.title: ➡️ Data Access Committee (DAC) and Data Access Compliance Office (DACO)
docs.text:
ARCHIMEDES is guided by a clear and trusted committee structure who perform portfolio-specific decision-making and advisory functions. These committees include the ARCHIMEDES Leadership, Joint Liaison, Community Partners, the Data Access Committee (DAC), and the Data Access Compliance Office (DACO).

The Data Access Committee (DAC) oversees requests from prospective Data Users to access Controlled Access data with the administrative support of the Data Access Committee Office (DACO). The ARCHIMEDES Data Access Committee (DAC) Terms of Reference can be found here. The DACO coordinates the DAC review of Data Access Requests and performs select monitoring functions regarding the ongoing commitments of authorized Data Users. For data contribution, the DACO reviews the documentation demonstrating ethical and legal authority, including informed consent materials and ethics approvals, provided by Data Contributors for compatibility with ARCHIMEDES policies before authorizing data contribution. The DACO also reviews requests for Data Contributor and Data User credentials

At this stage, an account creation can only be completed by a Principal Investigator (PI).

More information can be found on the FAQ and the Data Governance Framework.

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text: This section outlines the ARCHIMEDES Data Contribution process, including establishing a Data Contribution Agreement (DCA), creating an account, setting up a project, and submitting a Data Contribution Form (DCF). Guidance is provided for both Principal Investigators (PIs) and non-Principal Investigators. It also introduces the roles of the Data Access Committee (DAC) and the Data Access Compliance Office (DACO).

docs.title: Data Transfer
docs.text:
⚠️ Data submitted to ARCHIMEDES must comply with applicable privacy regulations and ethical approvals.

➡️ At a minimum, all data must be coded and have the necessary permissions for sharing. ➡️ All direct identifiers (e.g. names, social insurance numbers) are removed and replaced with a unique code, prior to contribution. ➡️ Data can be shared if it is de-identified (or local equivalent). This requires that all direct identifiers and most indirect identifiers must be removed or transformed to reduce the risk of re-identification to a very low level, in accordance with recognized standards and applicable legal and ethical requirements.

⚠️ Researchers are responsible for ensuring their data is prepared appropriately. ➡️ For more information, please refer to the De Identification Resources Hub and the Deidentification Tutorials. This includes structuring the data so it can be processed correctly by ARCHIMEDES (i.e., how the system organizes, validates, and imports data).

💡Tip: Health data may contain direct identifiers (e.g., names, health card numbers, exact addresses), indirect identifiers (e.g., date of birth, postal code, rare diagnoses), or embedded identifiers (e.g., metadata in imaging files or text within images). These elements must be carefully reviewed and either removed or modified to protect privacy.

➡️ According to ARCHIMEDES Data Submission, Date of Birth (DOB) is required as it supports data validation, structuring, and analysis (e.g., age consistency and longitudinal tracking). While the full format MM-DD-YYYY is preferred, users may alternatively provide MM-YYYY or YYYY when full DOB is not available or cannot be shared. ➡️ If the year is not available, users may provide age only. In these cases, the system applies a default setting that calculates a standardized DOB based on the available information (month/year or age) to ensure consistency within the database structure, while still treating DOB as an indirect identifier and maintaining privacy protections.

The sections below outline the recommended workflows and tools for each type of data transfer. Examples are provided at the end.

docs.title: Small Datasets 🟢
docs.text:
Small Datasets include individual files or modest data volumes size TBD that can be uploaded directly through the ARCHIMEDES, such as images or data files generated by laboratory instruments or analysis software.

  • ✅ Ideal for datasets under [size TBD]
  • ✅ No data preparation required
  • ✅ Upload directly through your browser
  • ✅ Simple, easy-to-use workflow

(feature to develop and should be the easiest user test case)

One of the key differences is the permissions associated with these modules and the user role that would be associated with accessing each module. Below is a screenshot of the various permissions for each module.

there are four key roles that would be associated with imaging tasks. * 1. MRI expert & LORIS developer: This user would have access to the DICOM Archive and the violated scans to understand more about the imaging ingestion and failures. * 2. MRI Technologist at the scanning site: This user would have access to the imaging uploader. This is where they would first upload the scan after a session using the imaging uploader -- upload tab, and they can then use the imaging uploader - browse tab to confirm the scan has been transmitted and the processing status. * 3. Study coordinators: This user would have access to the imaging browser. They need to interact with the scans per se, but it is good for them to be able to confirm that their participants' scans have been uploaded and ingested properly. * 4. Radiologist and other MRI QC expert: This user would have access to the imaging browser to access the scans at any site & they would also have access to the imaging QC permission to perform quality control on the images.

What is the difference between Imaging Brower and Imaging Uploader browser tab: The imaging uploader - browse tab provides you information about your recent upload, such as the progress of the imaging processing and where files are located. The imaging browser provides you a more general overview for coordinators. No details about the processing steps, but you have the general information of what was ingested and when as well as accessing the images.

What is the difference between the DICOM Archive and the Imaging Uploader Browser tab: The Imaging uploader browse tab provides you with an overview of the processing steps for the file you uploaded. The DICOM Archive keeps a copy of all the files that were submitted to LORIS, whether they were properly inserted in the database or if they faced an issue and were flagged as violated scans.

docs.title: BIDS Uploader
docs.text: TBD

docs.title: Instrument Manager
docs.text:
Click on Instrument Manager under the Admin tab to view the list of instruments available in ARCHIMEDES. This feature also lets you create new behavioural forms within your ARCHIMEDES database.

A new page will appear giving you the option to browse or upload instruments.

➡️ To browse

  • Use the filters to search for an instrument.
  • The table will show details about each instrument, including whether it is properly set up in the system.

Browse column descriptions:

  • Instrument: Name of the instrument
  • Instrument Type: Either PHP or Instrument Builder (LINST file)
  • Table Installed: Shows if the instrument’s data table exists (Exists or Missing)
  • Table Valid: Indicates if the database is correctly set up for the instrument
  • Pages Valid: Indicates if all required instrument pages are properly configured

➡️ To upload

If your ARCHIMEDES account has this feature enabled, you’ll see the Upload tab where you can add new instruments created with the Instrument Builder. * Click Browse to select a LINST file from your computer, * Click Install Instrument to add it to ARCHIMEDES.

ℹ️Note: After installing the instrument, you’ll need to manually assign it to the study before it can be used. This step is done through the back-end system.

docs.title: Electrophysiology
docs.text:
The Electrophysiology Browser module allows you to review, filter, annotate and download (upload to come) EEG recordings and metadata.

This module allows you to upload EEG recordings and browse past uploads.

In the Browse tab, you can use the Selection Filter section to search for existing uploads.

In the data table, you can click any link in the Upload Location column to download the uploaded files.

To upload recordings, click on the Upload tab. Select your file for upload and enter the required information about the recording. Your dataset should be BIDS-compliant.

docs.title: Large Datasets 🔵
docs.text: Large Datasets involve higher data volumes that are more efficiently transferred in bulk using secure file transfer (SFTP), typically following data preparation steps to ensure integrity and performance. If you have many files or files larger than TBD MB, follow this process to transfer everything securely and efficiently.

docs.title: Data preparation
docs.text:
The Principal Investigator (PI) is responsible for preparing the data before submission, ensuring it meets both the project’s specific requirements and the formatting standards required by ARCHIMEDES.

⚠️ Data submitted to ARCHIMEDES must comply with applicable privacy regulations and ethical approvals.

➡️ At a minimum, all data must be Coded Data and have the necessary permissions for sharing. ➡️ All direct identifiers (e.g. names, social insurance numbers) are removed and replaced with a unique code, prior to contribution. ➡️ Data can be shared if it is de-identified (or local equivalent). This requires that all direct identifiers and most indirect identifiers must be removed or transformed to reduce the risk of re-identification to a very low level, in accordance with recognized standards and applicable legal and ethical requirements.

⚠️ Researchers are responsible for ensuring their data is prepared appropriately. ➡️ More information can be found in our De Identification Resources Hub. This includes structuring the data so it can be processed correctly by ARCHIMEDES (i.e., how the system organizes, validates, and imports data).

💡Tip: Health data may contain direct identifiers (e.g., names, health card numbers, exact addresses), indirect identifiers (e.g., date of birth, postal code, rare diagnoses), or embedded identifiers (e.g., metadata in imaging files or text within images). These elements must be carefully reviewed and either removed or modified to protect privacy.

➡️ According to ARCHIMEDES Data Submission, Date of Birth (DOB) is required as it supports data validation, structuring, and analysis (e.g., age consistency and longitudinal tracking). While the full format MM-DD-YYYY is preferred, users may alternatively provide MM-YYYY or YYYY when full DOB is not available or cannot be shared. If the year is not available, users may provide age only. In these cases, the system applies a default setting that calculates a standardized DOB based on the available information (month/year or age) to ensure consistency within the database structure, while still treating DOB as an indirect identifier and maintaining privacy protections.

➡️ Please follow the ARCHIMEDES Data Submission guidelines to ensure your files meet the required structure and format.

docs.title: Transfer large datasets
docs.text:
🔐 Secure File Transfer (SFTP)

Use this method when you need to transfer large files or sensitive data securely. SFTP is commonly used when your institution requires encrypted data transfer.

Follow the official procedure to transfer all files at once.

(TBD - currently the uploads are performed on the backend)

docs.title: Custom Assisted Transfer 🛠️
docs.text: Custom Assisted Transfer is available for more complex or specialized requirements, with support from the ARCHIMEDES team.

docs.title: Cardio Neuro Mind Data Platform (CNMDP)
docs.text: As a CNMDP user, the data originates from a structured database. Data preparation and transfer are handled automatically on the back end through coordination between the CNMDP and ARCHIMEDES technical teams. Researchers are therefore only required to focus on data collection, while the technical teams manage the preparation and secure transfer of the data to the defined project site.

docs.title: Account creation
docs.text: Place holder - will CNNDP user create their own account?

docs.title: Data capture
docs.text:
The CNMDP supports diverse research data types using specialized tools, such as those for behavioral assessments, imaging, and biospecimen tracking. The tools listed below are the most frequently used across studies in the CNMDP.

  • REDCap – Behavioral and survey questionnaires; neuropsychological assessments
  • LabKey – Biological and biospecimen data management
  • XNAT
  • Imaging: MRI, CT, PET, fNIRS
  • Electrophysiology: EEG, EKG, MEG, eye-tracking
  • Wearables – High-frequency time-series data (sensor, wearable, physiological streams)
  • Superset

docs.title: Data preparation
docs.text:
The ARCHIMEDES clinical pipeline automates the process of importing REDCap study data into ARCHIMEDES. It handles instrument installation, candidate and session creation, and data ingestion in a single run. The following must be set up in ARCHIMEDES before running the data ingestion pipeline:

  • Project – Your study project must already exist in ARCHIMEDES (e.g., "CNMDP").
  • Site – The site where data was collected must already exist in LORIS (e.g., "University of Ottawa Heart Institute", “The Royal/IMHR”).
  • Cohort – The participant cohort must exist and be linked to the project (e.g., “Affected,” “Not-Affected,” “Control”)
  • Visits – Each REDCap event (e.g., visit_1_arm_1, visit_2_arm_1) must be registered as a visit in LORIS and linked to the project and cohort. The pipeline does not create visit definitions automatically.

All of the above will be created by the ARCHIMEDES technical team on the database.

💡Tip: Health data may contain direct identifiers (e.g., names, health card numbers, exact addresses), indirect identifiers (e.g., date of birth, postal code, rare diagnoses), or embedded identifiers (e.g., metadata in imaging files or text within images). These elements must be carefully reviewed and either removed or modified to protect privacy.

➡️ According to ARCHIMEDES Data Submission, Date of Birth (DOB) is required as it supports data validation, structuring, and analysis (e.g., age consistency and longitudinal tracking). While the full format MM-DD-YYYY is preferred, users may alternatively provide MM-YYYY or YYYY when full DOB is not available or cannot be shared. If the year is not available, users may provide age only. In these cases, the system applies a default setting that calculates a standardized DOB based on the available information (month/year or age) to ensure consistency within the database structure, while still treating DOB as an indirect identifier and maintaining privacy protections.

➡️ Data preparation by CNMDP technical team

The files will be placed in the expected folder structure:

{base_path}/{ProjectName}/
├── project.json
├── deidentified-raw/
│   └── clinical/
│       └── redcap_data.csv
├── documentation/
│   └── data_dictionary/
│       └── data_dict.csv
│

➡️ Data Dictionary File (documentation/data_dictionary/)

This is the REDCap data dictionary exported from your REDCap project. It defines all the instruments and their fields. The pipeline will use it to install instruments into ARCHIMEDES automatically.

➡️ For reference, visit the ARCHIMEDES Data Ingestion guidelines for behavioural data.

docs.title: Data transfer
docs.text:
To prepare data for ingestion into ARCHIMEDES, the data must be structured according to the specifications defined by the ARCHIMEDES ingestion pipelines (available as a reference). Each data type (e.g., imaging, behavioural, biospecimen...) must be prepared separately and follow the specific formatting requirements for that data type.

Data ingestion refers to the process of importing and organizing the data within the ARCHIMEDES platform so that it can be securely stored, managed, and accessed. This step is managed by the CNMDP and ARCHIMEDES technical teams and is therefore transparent to the user. No action is required from the user at this stage.

docs.title: Data preparation
docs.text:
The Principal Investigator (PI) is responsible for preparing the data before submission, ensuring it meets both the project’s specific requirements and the formatting standards required by ARCHIMEDES. Data submitted to ARCHIMEDES must comply with applicable privacy regulations and ethical approvals. In many cases this involves de-identifying or coding data with consent prior to submission, i.e that any information that could directly or indirectly identify an individual (such as names, IDs, or contact details) has been removed or replaced. More information can be found in our De Identification Resources Hub. This includes structuring the data so it can be processed correctly by ARCHIMEDES (i.e., how the system organizes, validates, and imports data).

➡️ Please follow the ARCHIMEDES Data submission guidelines to ensure your files meet the required structure and format.

docs.title: UOHI Data De-identification control and transfer process
docs.text:
The following section outlines the process for transferring data to ARCHIMEDES through UOHI. The workflow involves coordination between the Principal Investigator (PI), ARCHIMEDES, and UOHI teams to ensure data is securely reviewed, de-identified, and ingested.

➡️ Step 1: Project Setup (PI) 👤

  • Set up your project in ARCHIMEDES before initiating any data transfer
  • Confirmed you are on the approved PI list from UOHI
  • Create your ARCHIMEDES account
  • Create a new project
  • Complete and submit the Data Contribution Form (DCF)
  • Wait for DACO approval
  • Create your project in ARCHIMEDES by providing all required information

➡️ Step 2: Data Request & Review (ARCHIMEDES → UOHI) 📨

  • ARCHIMEDES initiates the data request based on your project information
  • ARCHIMEDES submits the Data Request Form to the UOHI Privacy Office
  • The Privacy Office reviews the request
  • Once approved, you will be contacted by the Privacy team to grant access to your data location

➡️ Step 3: De-identification (UOHI) 🔐

  • Your data is reviewed and prepared to meet privacy requirements
  • De-identification is processed from the granted access data storage location
  • Your data is reviewed by the appropriate team:
    • Imaging data → UOHI IT
    • Behavioral / clinical data/ non imaging data → UOHI Privacy Team
  • A final verification ensures all data is properly de-identified

➡️ Step 4: Data Transfer & Ingestion in ARCHIMEDES 📤

  • Your data is securely transferred and made available in ARCHIMEDES
  • Approved data are uploaded to the designated shared drive either by UOHI IT, UOHI Privacy, or the UOHI ARCHIMEDES team)
  • ARCHIMEDES accesses the data directly (no duplication required)
  • Data is ingested according to your project specifications
